docs.title: Bioinformatics File Formats Index
docs.text: A reference collection of file formats used in bioinformatics analysis pipelines, including indexing strategies and tool relationships.

docs.title: Sequence Data
docs.text: Format Extension Description Index FASTQ .fastq.gz Raw sequencing reads with quality scores None (not indexed) FASTA .fa, .fasta Nucleotide/protein sequences .fai (samtools faidx)

docs.title: Alignment Data
docs.text: Format Extension Description Index SAM .sam Text alignment format None (convert to BAM) BAM .bam Binary alignment (compressed) .bai (samtools index) or .csi CRAM .cram Reference-based compressed alignment .crai (samtools index)

docs.title: Variant Data
docs.text: Format Extension Description Index VCF .vcf.gz Variant calls (SNPs, indels, SVs) .tbi (tabix) or .csi (bcftools) BCF .bcf Binary VCF (faster I/O) .csi (bcftools index)

docs.title: Genomic Intervals / Annotation
docs.text: Format Extension Description Index BED .bed Genomic intervals (0-based) .tbi (tabix, after bgzip) GFF/GTF .gtf, .gff3 Gene annotation (1-based) .tbi (tabix, after bgzip) BigBed .bb Binary indexed BED Self-indexed

docs.title: Coverage / Signal Data
docs.text: Format Extension Description Index BigWig .bw Binary normalised coverage signal Self-indexed BedGraph .bedgraph Text coverage (BED + value) .tbi (tabix, after bgzip) WIG .wig Text wiggle (fixed/variable step) None (convert to BigWig)

docs.title: Indexing Summary
docs.text:
Quick reference for which index types apply to which formats:

Index Type Extension Created By Formats BAI .bai samtools index BAM CSI .csi samtools index -c / bcftools index BAM, VCF, BCF CRAI .crai samtools index CRAM TBI .tbi tabix -p <fmt> VCF, BED, GFF (bgzipped) FAI .fai samtools faidx FASTA Self-indexed (built-in) bedToBigBed / bamCoverage BigWig, BigBed

docs.title: Index Decision Tree
docs.text:
Is it alignment data?
├── BAM → .bai (or .csi for large chroms)
├── CRAM → .crai
└── SAM → convert to BAM first

Is it variant data?
├── VCF.gz → .tbi (tabix) or .csi (bcftools)
└── BCF → .csi (bcftools index)

Is it interval/annotation?
├── BED (bgzipped) → .tbi (tabix -p bed)
├── GFF (bgzipped) → .tbi (tabix -p gff)
└── BigBed → self-indexed

Is it coverage/signal?
├── BigWig → self-indexed
├── BedGraph (bgzipped) → .tbi
└── WIG → convert to BigWig

docs.title: Coordinate Systems
docs.text: System Formats Example (first 100bp of chr1) 0-based, half-open BED, BedGraph, BAM (internal) chr1 0 100 1-based, inclusive VCF, GFF/GTF, SAM (display), WIG chr1:1-100

docs.title: Format Conversion Paths
docs.text:
FASTQ → (align) → SAM → BAM → CRAM
                            ↓
                        VCF/BCF (variant calling)
                            ↓
                        BED (regions of interest)

BAM → BedGraph → BigWig (coverage)
BAM → BED (intervals)
BED → BigBed (indexed intervals)

docs.title: BAM Format (Binary Alignment/Map)
docs.text: Extension: .bam Type: Binary (BGZF-compressed) Specification: SAM/BAM Format Specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Binary, compressed, and indexed representation of SAM alignment data. The standard working format for aligned sequencing reads in virtually all NGS analysis pipelines. Supports random access via index files, enabling fast retrieval of reads in any genomic region.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: BAM is a BGZF (Blocked GZip Format) compressed binary encoding of SAM. It contains the same information as SAM (header + alignments) but is: - ~3-5× smaller than SAM - Supports random access when indexed - Required by most downstream tools

docs.title: BAI Index (.bai)
docs.text:
The standard BAM index. Required for random access (viewing specific regions, variant calling, coverage).

# Create BAI index (requires coordinate-sorted BAM)
samtools index aligned.sorted.bam
# produces: aligned.sorted.bam.bai

# Alternative: explicit output name
samtools index aligned.sorted.bam aligned.sorted.bai

Limitation: BAI supports reference sequences up to 2^29 (512 Mb). For larger chromosomes, use CSI.

docs.title: CSI Index (.csi)
docs.text:
Coordinate-Sorted Index with configurable bin sizes. Required for references with chromosomes >512Mb.

# Create CSI index
samtools index -c aligned.sorted.bam
# produces: aligned.sorted.bam.csi

# Or specify minimum shift
samtools index -c -m 14 aligned.sorted.bam

docs.title: Requirements for Indexing
docs.text:
1. BAM must be coordinate-sorted (samtools sort)
  2. Index must be in same directory as BAM (or discoverable)
  3. If BAM is modified, index must be regenerated

docs.title: Sort Orders
docs.text: Sort Command Use Case Coordinate samtools sort input.bam -o sorted.bam Most tools, indexing, viewing Name samtools sort -n input.bam -o namesorted.bam htseq-count, some PE tools

docs.title: Common Operations
docs.text:
# View BAM header
samtools view -H file.bam

# View reads in region (requires index)
samtools view file.bam chr1:1000000-2000000

# Count reads
samtools view -c file.bam

# Convert BAM to SAM
samtools view -h file.bam > file.sam

# Convert BAM to CRAM
samtools view -C -T reference.fa file.bam > file.cram

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context samtools sort Sorting SAM/BAM by coordinate or name samtools view SAM → BAM conversion STAR Direct sorted BAM output BWA + samtools Piped alignment Bowtie2 + samtools Piped alignment HISAT2 + samtools Piped alignment minimap2 + samtools Piped alignment Picard SortSam Sort/convert Picard MarkDuplicates Deduplication GATK ApplyBQSR Recalibrated BAM

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose samtools All BAM operations Picard Metrics, duplicate marking GATK Variant calling bcftools mpileup Variant calling FreeBayes Variant calling featureCounts Read quantification HTSeq-count Read quantification deepTools Coverage, heatmaps bedtools Coverage, intersections FastQC QC from aligned reads IGV Visualisation

docs.title: File Size Estimates
docs.text: Data Type Approximate BAM Size 30× WGS (human) ~50-100 GB RNA-seq (50M reads) ~3-5 GB ChIP-seq (20M reads) ~1-2 GB Exome (100× target) ~5-10 GB

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• SAM — text version (human-readable)
  • CRAM — more compressed (reference-based)
  • FASTQ — raw reads (pre-alignment)

docs.title: BCF Format (Binary Call Format)
docs.text: Extension: .bcf Type: Binary (BGZF-compressed) Specification: BCF2 Format

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Binary representation of VCF data. Provides faster I/O and smaller file sizes compared to text VCF, while containing identical information. Analogous to the BAM-SAM relationship. Particularly beneficial for large multi-sample VCF files.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: BCF is a binary encoding of the VCF data model: - Same header as VCF - Records stored as typed binary values - BGZF block compression - Supports random access via CSI index

docs.title: CSI Index (.csi)
docs.text:
# Index a BCF file
bcftools index variants.bcf       # → variants.bcf.csi

# Query a region
bcftools view variants.bcf chr1:1000000-2000000

docs.title: Creating BCF
docs.text:
# Convert VCF to BCF
bcftools view -Ob variants.vcf.gz -o variants.bcf
bcftools index variants.bcf

# Call variants directly to BCF
bcftools mpileup -f ref.fa sorted.bam | bcftools call -mv -Ob -o variants.bcf

# Any bcftools command with -Ob outputs BCF
bcftools filter -i 'QUAL>=20' variants.vcf.gz -Ob -o filtered.bcf

docs.title: Reading BCF
docs.text:
# View BCF as VCF text
bcftools view variants.bcf

# Convert BCF to compressed VCF
bcftools view -Oz variants.bcf -o variants.vcf.gz

# Query region
bcftools view variants.bcf chr1:1000000-2000000

# All bcftools operations work transparently on BCF
bcftools stats variants.bcf
bcftools query -f '%CHROM\t%POS\t%REF\t%ALT\n' variants.bcf

docs.title: Output Format Flags (bcftools)
docs.text: Flag Format -Ov Uncompressed VCF -Oz Compressed VCF (.vcf.gz) -Ob BCF -Ou Uncompressed BCF (for piping)

docs.title: BCF vs VCF Comparison
docs.text: Property VCF (.vcf.gz) BCF Size Larger ~10-20% smaller Read speed Slower (text parsing) Faster (binary) Index .tbi or .csi .csi Human-readable Yes (with zless) No Tool support Universal bcftools, GATK, htslib-based

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context bcftools Any command with -Ob

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose bcftools All VCF/BCF operations GATK Variant analysis Any htslib-based tool Transparent VCF/BCF support

docs.title: When to Use BCF
docs.text:
• Large multi-sample call sets (1000+ samples)
  • Pipelines with many bcftools operations (faster I/O)
  • When piping between bcftools commands (-Ou for uncompressed BCF)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• VCF — text version
  • BAM — similar binary concept for alignments

docs.title: BED Format (Browser Extensible Data)
docs.text: Extension: .bed Type: Text (tab-delimited) Specification: UCSC BED Format

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Simple tab-delimited format for representing genomic intervals (regions). Used for gene annotations, peaks (ChIP-seq), target regions (capture kits), blacklists, and any set of genomic coordinates. The foundation for interval arithmetic operations.

docs.title: Mandatory Fields (BED3)
docs.text:
chr1    1000    2000
chr1    3000    4000
chr2    5000    6000
Column Field Description 1 chrom Chromosome name 2 chromStart Start position (0-based) 3 chromEnd End position (exclusive)

Important: BED is 0-based, half-open — chr1 0 100 means bases 1-100 in 1-based coordinates.

docs.title: Extended Fields (BED6, BED12)
docs.text: Column Field Description 4 name Feature name 5 score Score (0-1000) 6 strand + or - 7 thickStart Display start (e.g., CDS start) 8 thickEnd Display end (e.g., CDS end) 9 itemRgb Display colour (R,G,B) 10 blockCount Number of blocks (exons) 11 blockSizes Comma-separated block sizes 12 blockStarts Comma-separated block starts

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# BED3 — simple regions
chr1    0       1000
chr1    2000    3000

# BED6 — with name, score, strand
chr1    0       1000    peak_1    500    +
chr1    2000    3000    peak_2    300    -

# BED12 — gene structure (exons as blocks)
chr1    11873   14409   NR_046018   0   +   11873   11873   0   3   354,109,1189,   0,739,1347,

docs.title: Tabix Index (.tbi)
docs.text:
For random access to bgzip-compressed BED:

# Sort, compress, and index
sort -k1,1 -k2,2n regions.bed | bgzip > regions.bed.gz
tabix -p bed regions.bed.gz

# Query a region
tabix regions.bed.gz chr1:1000000-2000000

docs.title: Requirements for Indexing
docs.text:
1. File must be sorted by chromosome and position
  2. Must be bgzip-compressed (not gzip)
  3. Chromosome sort order must be consistent

docs.title: Coordinate System Note
docs.text: Format System Example (first 100 bases of chr1) BED 0-based, half-open chr1 0 100 VCF/GFF/SAM 1-based, inclusive chr1:1-100

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context bedtools All interval operations bedtools bamtobed BAM → BED conversion Peak callers (MACS2, etc.) ChIP-seq/ATAC-seq peaks UCSC tools Annotation downloads

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose bedtools Interval arithmetic tabix Region queries samtools Region specification (-L) GATK Target intervals deepTools Region matrices featureCounts Custom regions (SAF) FreeBayes Target regions (-t) IGV Visualisation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• GFF/GTF — richer annotation format
  • BedGraph — BED with coverage scores
  • BigBed — indexed binary BED

docs.title: BedGraph Format
docs.text: Extension: .bedgraph, .bg Type: Text (tab-delimited) Specification: UCSC BedGraph

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: BED-like format for displaying continuous-valued data (genome coverage, signal intensity) as a genome browser track. Each line specifies a genomic interval and an associated value. More flexible than WIG for non-uniform intervals.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Four columns, tab-delimited:

chromA  chromStartA  chromEndA  dataValueA

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
chr1    0       1000    1.5
chr1    1000    2000    3.2
chr1    2000    2500    0.0
chr1    2500    3000    2.1
Column Description 1 Chromosome 2 Start (0-based) 3 End (exclusive) 4 Data value (float)

docs.title: Coordinate System
docs.text: BedGraph uses 0-based, half-open coordinates (same as BED).

docs.title: Tabix Index
docs.text:
# Sort and compress
sort -k1,1 -k2,2n coverage.bedgraph | bgzip > coverage.bedgraph.gz

# Create tabix index
tabix -p bed coverage.bedgraph.gz

# Query region
tabix coverage.bedgraph.gz chr1:1000000-2000000

docs.title: Convert to BigWig (recommended for large files)
docs.text:
# Generate chromosome sizes
cut -f1,2 reference.fa.fai > chrom.sizes

# Convert to BigWig (self-indexed, smaller, faster)
bedGraphToBigWig coverage.bedgraph chrom.sizes coverage.bw

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context bedtools genomecov -bg Coverage from BAM/BED Various signal processing tools Normalised scores

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose UCSC bedGraphToBigWig Convert to BigWig tabix Region queries bedtools Interval operations Genome browsers Track display

docs.title: BedGraph vs BigWig
docs.text: Property BedGraph BigWig Type Text Binary Indexed Via tabix Self-indexed Random access With tabix Built-in File size Larger Smaller Editable Yes No Browser display Must convert Direct

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BigWig — indexed binary version (preferred for production)
  • WIG — alternative text coverage format
  • BED — interval format (without values)

docs.title: BigBed Format
docs.text: Extension: .bb, .bigbed Type: Binary (indexed) Specification: UCSC BigBed

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Binary indexed version of BED format. Enables fast random access to interval data in genome browsers without loading entire files. Used for large annotation datasets (peaks, repeats, regulatory elements) that need efficient display.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: BigBed files contain: - Compressed BED records - Built-in R-tree spatial index - Summary statistics at multiple zoom levels - AutoSQL schema (field descriptions)

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text: BigBed files are self-indexed — no separate index file needed. Random access is built into the format.

docs.title: Creating BigBed
docs.text:
# Sort BED file
sort -k1,1 -k2,2n regions.bed > sorted.bed

# Create chromosome sizes file
cut -f1,2 reference.fa.fai > chrom.sizes

# Convert to BigBed
bedToBigBed sorted.bed chrom.sizes output.bb

# With extra fields (custom BED format)
bedToBigBed -type=bed6 sorted.bed chrom.sizes output.bb

# BED12 (gene structures)
bedToBigBed -type=bed12 genes.bed chrom.sizes genes.bb

docs.title: Reading BigBed
docs.text:
# Convert back to BED
bigBedToBed input.bb output.bed

# Extract specific region
bigBedToBed -chrom=chr1 -start=1000000 -end=2000000 input.bb region.bed

# Summary info
bigBedInfo input.bb

# Summary statistics
bigBedSummary input.bb chr1 1000000 2000000 10

docs.title: BigBed vs BED
docs.text: Property BigBed BED (bgzipped+tabix) Type Binary Text Index Built-in Separate .tbi Browser support Native Limited File size Smaller Larger Editable No Yes Creation bedToBigBed bgzip + tabix

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context UCSC bedToBigBed BED → BigBed conversion ENCODE pipeline Peak file delivery

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose UCSC Genome Browser Track display IGV Visualisation UCSC bigBedToBed Convert to text BED

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BED — text interval format
  • BigWig — similar binary format for continuous data

docs.title: BigWig Format
docs.text: Extension: .bw, .bigwig Type: Binary (indexed) Specification: UCSC BigWig

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Binary indexed format for dense, continuous signal data across a genome. Stores numerical values (coverage, scores, signal intensity) at specific genomic positions or intervals. Enables fast display in genome browsers without loading entire datasets.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: BigWig is a compressed, indexed binary format that supports: - Fast random access to any genomic region - Multiple zoom levels for efficient display at different scales - Both fixed-step and variable-step data - Built-in summary statistics

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text: BigWig files are self-indexed — no separate index file is needed. The R-tree index is embedded within the file, enabling instant random access to any region.

docs.title: From BAM (most common)
docs.text:
# Using deepTools bamCoverage (recommended)
bamCoverage -b sorted.bam -o coverage.bw \
    --normalizeUsing RPKM --binSize 10 -p 8

# Using deepTools bamCompare (ChIP/input ratio)
bamCompare -b1 chip.bam -b2 input.bam -o log2ratio.bw -p 8

# Using deepTools with different normalisations
bamCoverage -b sorted.bam -o coverage.bw --normalizeUsing CPM
bamCoverage -b sorted.bam -o coverage.bw --normalizeUsing BPM
bamCoverage -b sorted.bam -o coverage.bw --normalizeUsing RPGC --effectiveGenomeSize 2913022398

docs.title: From BedGraph
docs.text:
# Convert BedGraph to BigWig (UCSC tools)
bedGraphToBigWig coverage.bedgraph chrom.sizes output.bw

# Generate chrom.sizes
samtools faidx reference.fa
cut -f1,2 reference.fa.fai > chrom.sizes

docs.title: From WIG
docs.text:
# Convert WIG to BigWig
wigToBigWig input.wig chrom.sizes output.bw

docs.title: Reading BigWig
docs.text:
# View signal in a region (UCSC bigWigSummary)
bigWigSummary coverage.bw chr1 1000000 2000000 10

# Extract to BedGraph
bigWigToBedGraph coverage.bw output.bedgraph

# Extract specific region
bigWigToBedGraph -chrom=chr1 -start=1000000 -end=2000000 coverage.bw region.bedgraph

# Using deepTools (compute matrix for plotting)
computeMatrix reference-point -S coverage.bw -R genes.bed -o matrix.gz

docs.title: Normalisation Methods (deepTools)
docs.text: Method Description RPKM Reads Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads CPM Counts Per Million mapped reads BPM Bins Per Million (like TPM for bins) RPGC Reads Per Genomic Content (1× coverage) None Raw read counts

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context deepTools bamCoverage BAM → normalised BigWig deepTools bamCompare Ratio BigWig UCSC bedGraphToBigWig BedGraph → BigWig UCSC wigToBigWig WIG → BigWig

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose deepTools computeMatrix Signal matrices for heatmaps deepTools plotHeatmap/plotProfile Visualisation IGV Genome browser display UCSC Genome Browser Track display UCSC bigWigSummary Summary statistics UCSC bigWigToBedGraph Convert back to text

docs.title: BigWig vs BedGraph vs WIG
docs.text: Property BigWig BedGraph WIG Type Binary Text Text Indexed Built-in Via tabix No Random access Fast With tabix No File size Small Large Large Genome browser Direct Must convert Must convert Editability No Yes Yes

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BedGraph — text-based coverage format
  • WIG — text wiggle format
  • BigBed — similar concept for interval data
  • BAM — source alignment data

docs.title: CRAM Format
docs.text: Extension: .cram Type: Binary (reference-based compression) Specification: CRAM Format Specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Highly compressed alignment format that uses reference-based compression to achieve 30-60% size reduction compared to BAM. Stores only the differences between reads and the reference genome, making it ideal for long-term archival of sequencing data.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text: CRAM encodes alignment data by: 1. Storing differences from reference rather than full sequences 2. Using columnar storage (fields compressed separately) 3. Supporting multiple codecs (gzip, bzip2, lzma, rans) 4. Embedding reference sequence checksums for integrity

docs.title: CRAI Index (.crai)
docs.text:
CRAM index file enables random access by genomic region.

# Create CRAI index
samtools index aligned.cram
# produces: aligned.cram.crai

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
1. CRAM must be coordinate-sorted
  2. Reference FASTA must be available (for reading)
  3. Reference must match the one used during CRAM creation

docs.title: Creating CRAM
docs.text:
# Convert BAM to CRAM
samtools view -C -T reference.fa sorted.bam -o aligned.cram

# Convert SAM to CRAM
samtools view -C -T reference.fa aligned.sam -o aligned.cram

# Sort and convert in one step
samtools sort --output-fmt cram --reference reference.fa input.sam -o sorted.cram

# Index the CRAM
samtools index aligned.cram

docs.title: Reading CRAM
docs.text:
# View CRAM (requires reference)
samtools view -T reference.fa aligned.cram

# View specific region
samtools view -T reference.fa aligned.cram chr1:1000000-2000000

# Convert CRAM back to BAM
samtools view -b -T reference.fa aligned.cram -o converted.bam

# Use REF_PATH environment for reference cache
export REF_PATH=/path/to/ref_cache/%2s/%2s/%s
samtools view aligned.cram chr1:1000-2000

docs.title: Reference Management
docs.text:
CRAM files require the original reference to decode. Set up reference paths:

# Set reference cache directory
export REF_PATH="$HOME/.cache/hts-ref/%2s/%2s/%s"
export REF_CACHE="$HOME/.cache/hts-ref/%2s/%2s/%s"

# Or specify per-command
samtools view -T reference.fa file.cram

docs.title: Compression Comparison
docs.text: Format Relative Size Random Access Reference Needed SAM 100% (baseline) No No BAM ~25-30% Yes (.bai/.csi) No CRAM ~15-20% Yes (.crai) Yes (for decode)

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context samtools view -C BAM/SAM → CRAM conversion samtools sort Direct CRAM output

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose samtools All alignment operations GATK Variant calling Picard Metrics and validation IGV Visualisation

docs.title: When to Use CRAM
docs.text:
• Long-term archival storage (space savings significant at scale)
  • Submitting to public repositories (EBI/ENA prefers CRAM)
  • When reference genome is stable and available

docs.title: When to Use BAM Instead
docs.text:
• Active analysis (avoid reference dependency)
  • Pipelines where reference may not be readily available
  • When compatibility with older tools is needed

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BAM — standard working format
  • SAM — human-readable text format

docs.title: FASTA Format
docs.text: Extension: .fa, .fasta, .fna (nucleotide), .faa (amino acid) Type: Text Specification: Pearson & Lipman 1988

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Simple text format for representing nucleotide or protein sequences. Used for reference genomes, transcriptomes, protein databases, and any sequence data where quality scores are not needed.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
>SEQUENCE_ID optional description
SEQUENCE_DATA
SEQUENCE_DATA_CONTINUED

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
>chr1 Homo sapiens chromosome 1
NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNGATCACAGGTCTATCACCC
TGTAACTTAAACCCTTACTTAAGCTTAAAGAAAGGGCTTGCATTTACCCTTTTG
>chr2 Homo sapiens chromosome 2
NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNACCCAAAGCTCAGCCTTGTTTCTTGTAACT

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Component Description > Header line prefix Sequence ID First word after > (no spaces) Description Remainder of header line (optional) Sequence One or more lines of sequence data (typically wrapped at 60-80 chars)

docs.title: FASTA Index (.fai)
docs.text:
Created by samtools faidx, enables rapid random access to subsequences without reading entire file.

# Create index
samtools faidx reference.fa

# Extract region using index
samtools faidx reference.fa chr1:1000-2000

The .fai file is tab-delimited:

Column Content 1 Sequence name 2 Sequence length 3 Byte offset of first base 4 Bases per line 5 Bytes per line

docs.title: Sequence Dictionary (.dict)
docs.text:
Required by GATK/Picard. Created by picard CreateSequenceDictionary or samtools dict.

samtools dict reference.fa > reference.dict
picard CreateSequenceDictionary R=reference.fa O=reference.dict

docs.title: BLAST Database Index
docs.text:
For similarity searching:

makeblastdb -in sequences.fa -dbtype nucl -out mydb

Produces: .nhr, .nin, .nsq (nucleotide) or .phr, .pin, .psq (protein)

docs.title: BWA Index
docs.text:
For short-read alignment:

bwa index reference.fa

Produces: .amb, .ann, .bwt, .pac, .sa

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context Genome assemblies Reference genomes bcftools consensus Consensus from VCF bedtools getfasta Extracted regions SeqKit Converted/filtered sequences samtools faidx Extracted subsequences

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose BWA Reference for alignment Bowtie2 Reference for alignment STAR Genome index building BLAST Database/query sequences HMMER Sequence databases MUSCLE Multiple alignment input MAFFT Multiple alignment input Kallisto Transcriptome index Salmon Transcriptome index samtools Reference for pileup SeqKit Sequence manipulation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• FASTQ — sequences with quality scores
  • SAM/BAM — aligned sequences reference this format

docs.title: FASTQ Format
docs.text: Extension: .fastq, .fq, .fastq.gz, .fq.gz Type: Text (often gzip-compressed) Encoding: ASCII Specification: Cock et al. 2010, NAR 38:1767-1771

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Stores raw sequencing reads with per-base quality scores. The standard output format from Illumina, PacBio, and Oxford Nanopore sequencing platforms. Each record contains a sequence identifier, the nucleotide sequence, and base-call quality values.

docs.title: Structure
docs.text:
Each record consists of exactly 4 lines:

@SEQ_ID optional_description
SEQUENCE
+
QUALITY

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@SRR001666.1 071112_SLXA-EAS1_s_7:5:1:817:345 length=72
GGGTGATGGCCGCTGCCGATGGCGTCAAATCCCACCAAGTTACCCTTAACAACTTAAGGGTTTTCAAATAGA
+
IIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIII9IG9ICIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIIDIIIIIII>IIIIII/

docs.title: Fields
docs.text: Line Content 1 @ + Sequence ID + optional description 2 Raw nucleotide sequence (A, C, G, T, N) 3 + (optionally followed by ID again) 4 Quality scores (same length as line 2)

docs.title: Quality Encoding
docs.text:
Quality scores represent the probability of an incorrect base call:

$$Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{error})$$

Encoding Offset Range Platforms Phred+33 (Sanger) 33 0-41 Modern Illumina, PacBio, Nanopore Phred+64 (Illumina 1.3-1.7) 64 0-41 Legacy Illumina (pre-2011)

ASCII character I (ASCII 73) with Phred+33 = quality score 40 (99.99% accuracy).

docs.title: Paired-End Reads
docs.text:
Paired-end data is typically stored in two files with matching read order: - sample_R1.fastq.gz — Forward reads (Read 1) - sample_R2.fastq.gz — Reverse reads (Read 2)

Read names must match between files. Some tools accept interleaved format (R1 and R2 alternating in one file).

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text: FASTQ files are not typically indexed. For random access, convert to BAM (after alignment) or use tools like seqkit faidx on FASTA.

docs.title: Compression
docs.text:
• Always store compressed (.fastq.gz) — typically 3-5× size reduction
  • Use pigz for parallel compression: pigz -p 8 reads.fastq

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context Sequencing platforms Primary output fastp Trimmed/filtered reads Trimmomatic Trimmed reads Cutadapt Adapter-removed reads samtools fastq Extracted from BAM SeqKit Filtered/transformed reads

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose FastQC Quality assessment BWA Alignment Bowtie2 Alignment STAR RNA-seq alignment HISAT2 RNA-seq alignment minimap2 Long-read alignment Kallisto Pseudoalignment Salmon Transcript quantification SeqKit Manipulation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• FASTA — sequence without quality scores
  • BAM — aligned reads (downstream of FASTQ)

docs.title: GFF/GTF Format (General Feature Format / Gene Transfer Format)
docs.text: Extension: .gff, .gff3, .gtf Type: Text (tab-delimited) Specification: GFF3 Spec | GTF/GFF2.5

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Describes genomic features (genes, transcripts, exons, CDS) with hierarchical relationships. Used for genome annotation. GTF is a strict subset of GFF used primarily by Ensembl and many RNA-seq tools.

docs.title: Structure (GFF3)
docs.text:
Nine tab-delimited columns:

Col Field Description 1 seqid Chromosome/scaffold 2 source Annotation source (e.g., "ensembl") 3 type Feature type (gene, mRNA, exon, CDS) 4 start Start position (1-based, inclusive) 5 end End position (1-based, inclusive) 6 score Score (or .) 7 strand +, -, or . 8 phase Reading frame for CDS (0, 1, 2, or .) 9 attributes Semicolon-separated key=value pairs

docs.title: GFF3 Example
docs.text:
chr1    ensembl gene    11869   14409   .   +   .   ID=ENSG00000223972;Name=DDX11L1;biotype=transcribed_unprocessed_pseudogene
chr1    ensembl mRNA    11869   14409   .   +   .   ID=ENST00000456328;Parent=ENSG00000223972;Name=DDX11L1-201
chr1    ensembl exon    11869   12227   .   +   .   Parent=ENST00000456328
chr1    ensembl exon    12613   12721   .   +   .   Parent=ENST00000456328
chr1    ensembl exon    13221   14409   .   +   .   Parent=ENST00000456328

docs.title: GTF Example
docs.text:
chr1    ensembl gene    11869   14409   .   +   .   gene_id "ENSG00000223972"; gene_name "DDX11L1";
chr1    ensembl transcript  11869   14409   .   +   .   gene_id "ENSG00000223972"; transcript_id "ENST00000456328";
chr1    ensembl exon    11869   12227   .   +   .   gene_id "ENSG00000223972"; transcript_id "ENST00000456328"; exon_number "1";
chr1    ensembl exon    12613   12721   .   +   .   gene_id "ENSG00000223972"; transcript_id "ENST00000456328"; exon_number "2";
chr1    ensembl exon    13221   14409   .   +   .   gene_id "ENSG00000223972"; transcript_id "ENST00000456328"; exon_number "3";

docs.title: GFF3 vs GTF Differences
docs.text: Feature GFF3 GTF (GFF2.5) Hierarchy Explicit ID/Parent Implicit via gene_id/transcript_id Attributes key=value key "value"; Multi-value Parent=id1,id2 Not supported Directives ##gff-version 3, ### None Feature types Ontology-based (SO) Fixed set (gene, transcript, exon, CDS)

docs.title: Tabix Index
docs.text:
# Sort, compress, and index GFF3
sort -k1,1 -k4,4n annotation.gff3 | bgzip > annotation.gff3.gz
tabix -p gff annotation.gff3.gz

# Query region
tabix annotation.gff3.gz chr1:11000-15000

docs.title: Coordinate System
docs.text:
GFF/GTF uses 1-based, inclusive coordinates (same as VCF, different from BED).

Format chr1, first 100 bases GFF/GTF chr1 ... 1 ... 100 BED chr1 0 100

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context Genome annotation databases Ensembl, GENCODE, RefSeq, UCSC Gene prediction tools Augustus, GeneMark HISAT2 extract scripts Splice site extraction from GTF

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose STAR Splice junction annotation for index HISAT2 Splice site extraction featureCounts Gene/exon counting HTSeq-count Gene counting bedtools Interval operations tabix Region queries Kallisto Genome-BAM output

docs.title: Common Operations
docs.text:
# Extract gene names from GTF
awk '$3 == "gene"' annotation.gtf | grep -o 'gene_name "[^"]*"' | sort -u

# Convert GTF to BED (genes)
awk '$3 == "gene" {print $1"\t"$4-1"\t"$5"\t"$10"\t.\t"$7}' annotation.gtf | tr -d '";' > genes.bed

# Count features by type
awk '{print $3}' annotation.gtf | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BED — simpler interval format
  • FASTA — sequence data referenced by GFF

docs.title: SAM Format (Sequence Alignment/Map)
docs.text: Extension: .sam Type: Text (tab-delimited) Specification: SAM/BAM Format Specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Tab-delimited text format for storing aligned sequencing reads against a reference. Human-readable counterpart to BAM. Contains alignment coordinates, mapping quality, CIGAR strings describing alignment operations, and optional tags for additional metadata.

docs.title: Header Section (lines starting with @)
docs.text:
@HD     VN:1.6  SO:coordinate
@SQ     SN:chr1 LN:248956422
@SQ     SN:chr2 LN:242193529
@RG     ID:sample1      SM:sample1      PL:ILLUMINA     LB:lib1
@PG     ID:bwa  PN:bwa  VN:0.7.17       CL:bwa mem -t 8 ref.fa r1.fq r2.fq
Tag Description @HD Header line (version, sort order) @SQ Reference sequence dictionary @RG Read group @PG Program used

docs.title: Alignment Section
docs.text:
Each line represents one read alignment with 11 mandatory fields:

Col Field Description 1 QNAME Read name 2 FLAG Bitwise flag (see BAM flags) 3 RNAME Reference sequence name 4 POS 1-based leftmost mapping position 5 MAPQ Mapping quality (Phred-scaled) 6 CIGAR Alignment operations 7 RNEXT Mate reference name (= if same) 8 PNEXT Mate position 9 TLEN Template length (insert size) 10 SEQ Read sequence 11 QUAL Base quality (Phred+33)

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
@HD VN:1.6  SO:coordinate
@SQ SN:chr1 LN:248956422
read001 99  chr1    10000   60  76M =   10200   276 ACGTACGT... IIIIIII...  NM:i:0  MD:Z:76
read001 147 chr1    10200   60  76M =   10000   -276    TGCATGCA... IIIIIII...  NM:i:1  MD:Z:50A25

docs.title: CIGAR String
docs.text:
Op Description M Alignment match (or mismatch) I Insertion to reference D Deletion from reference N Skipped region (intron in RNA-seq) S Soft clipping (present in SEQ) H Hard clipping (not in SEQ) = Sequence match X Sequence mismatch

Example: 50M2I24M = 50 matches, 2bp insertion, 24 matches

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text:
SAM files are not indexed. Convert to BAM for indexed access:

samtools view -bS file.sam | samtools sort -o sorted.bam
samtools index sorted.bam

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context BWA Default alignment output Bowtie2 Default alignment output HISAT2 Default alignment output minimap2 With -a flag samtools view BAM → SAM conversion

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose samtools Convert to BAM, filter, stats Picard Validation, metrics featureCounts Read counting HTSeq-count Read counting

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• SAM is inefficient for storage — always convert to BAM or CRAM for archival
  • Useful for debugging and piping between tools
  • Some tools output SAM to stdout for piping: bwa mem ref.fa r1.fq r2.fq | samtools sort -o sorted.bam

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BAM — binary, compressed, indexed version
  • CRAM — reference-based compressed version

docs.title: VCF Format (Variant Call Format)
docs.text: Extension: .vcf, .vcf.gz Type: Text (tab-delimited), often bgzip-compressed Specification: VCF 4.3 Specification

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Standard format for representing genomic variants (SNPs, indels, structural variants) discovered by variant calling. Contains variant positions, reference/alternate alleles, quality scores, filter status, and per-sample genotype information.

docs.title: Header (lines starting with ##)
docs.text:
##fileformat=VCFv4.3
##FILTER=<ID=PASS,Description="All filters passed">
##INFO=<ID=DP,Number=1,Type=Integer,Description="Total Depth">
##INFO=<ID=AF,Number=A,Type=Float,Description="Allele Frequency">
##FORMAT=<ID=GT,Number=1,Type=String,Description="Genotype">
##FORMAT=<ID=DP,Number=1,Type=Integer,Description="Read Depth">
##FORMAT=<ID=GQ,Number=1,Type=Integer,Description="Genotype Quality">
##contig=<ID=chr1,length=248956422>
#CHROM  POS ID  REF ALT QUAL    FILTER  INFO    FORMAT  SAMPLE1 SAMPLE2

docs.title: Data Columns
docs.text: Col Field Description 1 CHROM Chromosome 2 POS 1-based position 3 ID Variant identifier (rsID or .) 4 REF Reference allele 5 ALT Alternate allele(s), comma-separated 6 QUAL Phred-scaled variant quality 7 FILTER Filter status (PASS or filter name) 8 INFO Semicolon-separated key=value annotations 9 FORMAT Colon-separated genotype field keys 10+ SAMPLES Per-sample genotype data

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
#CHROM  POS ID  REF ALT QUAL    FILTER  INFO    FORMAT  SAMPLE1
chr1    10177   rs367896724 A   AC  100 PASS    DP=50;AF=0.45   GT:DP:GQ    0/1:48:99
chr1    10235   .   T   TA  50  PASS    DP=30;AF=0.20   GT:DP:GQ    0/0:28:85
chr1    10352   rs145072688 T   TA  200 PASS    DP=100;AF=0.60  GT:DP:GQ    1/1:95:99

docs.title: Genotype Field
docs.text: Value Meaning 0/0 Homozygous reference 0/1 Heterozygous 1/1 Homozygous alternate 1/2 Heterozygous (two alt alleles) ./. Missing genotype 0 | 1 Phased heterozygous

docs.title: TBI Index (.tbi)
docs.text:
Standard tabix index for compressed VCF:

# Compress with bgzip
bgzip variants.vcf        # → variants.vcf.gz

# Index with tabix
tabix -p vcf variants.vcf.gz   # → variants.vcf.gz.tbi

# Query a region
tabix variants.vcf.gz chr1:1000000-2000000

docs.title: CSI Index (.csi)
docs.text:
For genomes with chromosomes >512Mb:

# Create CSI index
bcftools index variants.vcf.gz          # → .csi (default in bcftools)
tabix --csi variants.vcf.gz             # → .csi with tabix

# Or explicitly
bcftools index --csi variants.vcf.gz
bcftools index --tbi variants.vcf.gz    # force .tbi

docs.title: Index Comparison
docs.text: Index Extension Max Chrom Size Tool TBI .tbi 512 Mb tabix CSI .csi Unlimited bcftools index, tabix --csi

docs.title: gVCF (Genomic VCF)
docs.text:
Extended VCF that includes non-variant (reference) blocks. Used in GATK joint genotyping workflows.

chr1    10000   .   A   <NON_REF>   .   .   END=10100   GT:DP:GQ    0/0:30:90
chr1    10177   rs367896724 A   AC,<NON_REF>    100 .   DP=50   GT:DP:GQ    0/1:48:99

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context GATK HaplotypeCaller Germline variant calling GATK Mutect2 Somatic variant calling bcftools call Variant calling FreeBayes Bayesian variant calling bcftools filter/view Filtered variants

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose bcftools Filter, query, annotate, merge GATK Joint genotyping, filtering VCFtools Population statistics, filtering tabix Region queries PLINK GWAS import bedtools Interval operations IGV Visualisation

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BCF — binary VCF (faster I/O)
  • BAM — aligned reads (upstream of VCF)
  • BED — genomic intervals

docs.title: WIG Format (Wiggle)
docs.text: Extension: .wig Type: Text Specification: UCSC Wiggle Format

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Text format for displaying dense, continuous data (coverage, conservation scores, GC content) as a track in genome browsers. Supports both fixed-step (uniform spacing) and variable-step (irregular positions) data.

docs.title: Fixed-Step (uniform intervals)
docs.text:
fixedStep chrom=chr1 start=1 step=10 span=10
0.5
0.8
1.2
0.9

All values are equally spaced. step = distance between values, span = size of each value.

docs.title: Variable-Step (irregular positions)
docs.text:
variableStep chrom=chr1 span=10
1000    0.5
1050    0.8
1200    1.2
2000    0.9

Each line specifies position and value.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description chrom Chromosome name start Start position (1-based) step Distance between values (fixedStep) span Size of each feature (default: 1)

docs.title: Indexing
docs.text:
WIG files are not indexed. For random access, convert to BigWig:

# Convert WIG to indexed BigWig
wigToBigWig input.wig chrom.sizes output.bw

docs.title: Tools That Create This Format
docs.text: Tool Context Various coverage tools Legacy format output Conservation pipelines PhyloP, PhastCons scores

docs.title: Tools That Read This Format
docs.text: Tool Purpose UCSC wigToBigWig Convert to BigWig UCSC Genome Browser Display (prefers BigWig)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• WIG is largely replaced by BigWig for most applications
  • Convert to BigWig for efficient storage and access
  • Use BedGraph instead if you need per-region (non-uniform) coverage

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• BigWig — indexed binary version (preferred)
  • BedGraph — alternative text coverage format

docs.title: Analysis Processes
docs.text:
Reference pages for bioinformatics analysis workflows and processes, including sequencing, alignment, and downstream analysis.

Process Description Sequencing Overview of the complete sequencing workflow from sample preparation to raw data generation Alignment Detailed workflow of mapping reads to reference genomes and post-alignment processing

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The alignment process maps sequencing reads (typically in FASTQ format) to a reference genome or transcriptome. This is a critical step in most NGS analysis pipelines, enabling variant calling, quantification, and downstream analyses.

docs.title: Pre-alignment Steps
docs.text:
1. Reference preparation
  2. Genome indexing using tools like BWA index or Bowtie2-build
  3. Transcriptome indexing (for RNA-seq)
  4. Read preprocessing
  5. Quality filtering (FastQC, fastp)
  6. Adapter trimming (Cutadapt)
  7. Length filtering
  8. Quality control
  9. Read statistics (number, length distribution)
  10. Quality score assessment

docs.title: Short-read Aligners
docs.text: Tool Algorithm Best For BWA Burrows-Wheeler Transform General short-read DNA alignment Bowtie2 FM-index Fast, memory-efficient alignment HISAT2 Graph-based Splice-aware RNA-seq alignment STAR Split alignment Ultrafast splice-aware alignment

docs.title: Long-read Aligners
docs.text: Tool Algorithm Best For minimap2 Pairwise alignment Long-read alignment ngmlr Long-read alignment PacBio and Oxford Nanopore

docs.title: Alignment Parameters
docs.text:
• Mapping quality threshold (MAPQ)
  • Maximum mismatches allowed
  • Seed length for alignment
  • Gap penalties for indels
  • Splice site recognition (for RNA-seq)

docs.title: Post-alignment Processing
docs.text:
1. Sorting (coordinate-sorted BAM)
  2. Indexing (BAI or CSI index)
  3. Duplicate marking (Picard MarkDuplicates)
  4. Quality filtering (remove low MAPQ reads)
  5. Coverage calculation

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text:
• BAM/CRAM (aligned reads with alignment information)
  • BAI/CSI (index files for random access)
  • Metrics files (alignment statistics)
  • Unmapped reads (FASTQ format)

docs.title: Tools Involved
docs.text:
• BWA - Standard DNA short-read alignment
  • Bowtie2 - Fast short-read alignment
  • STAR - RNA-seq splice-aware alignment
  • HISAT2 - Graph-based RNA-seq alignment
  • minimap2 - Long-read alignment
  • samtools - BAM manipulation and sorting
  • Picard - Duplicate marking and metrics

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The sequencing process refers to the workflow of preparing and analyzing DNA or RNA samples for high-throughput sequencing technologies. This encompasses sample preparation, library construction, sequencing, and initial data processing.

docs.title: Sample Preparation
docs.text:
• DNA/RNA extraction from biological samples (cells, tissues, blood)
  • Quality control of extracted nucleic acids (concentration, integrity)
  • Fragmentation of DNA/RNA to appropriate sizes for sequencing
  • Adapter ligation for library construction
  • PCR amplification (if needed) to generate sufficient material

docs.title: Library Construction
docs.text:
• End repair and A-tailing of DNA fragments
  • Adapter addition for sequencing platform compatibility
  • Size selection using gel electrophoresis or bead-based methods
  • Indexing (barcoding) for multiplexing samples
  • Quality control of library constructs

docs.title: Sequencing Technologies
docs.text: Technology Read Length Platform Use Case Illumina (NextSeq, NovaSeq) 50-300bp Short-read WGS, WES, RNA-seq PacBio SMRT 1-20kb Long-read De novo assembly, structural variants Oxford Nanopore 100bp-100kb Long-read Real-time, portable, long reads 454 100-1000bp Pyrosequencing Targeted sequencing

docs.title: Quality Control Steps
docs.text:
• Base calling from raw instrument output
  • Quality filtering of reads (Phred scores)
  • Adapter trimming
  • Length filtering
  • Duplicate removal (if applicable)

docs.title: Data Output
docs.text:
• Raw reads in FASTQ format
  • Quality metrics (FastQC reports)
  • Index files for multiplexed samples
  • Sample metadata (library prep details, sequencing parameters)

docs.title: Tools Involved
docs.text:
• FastQC - Quality assessment
  • fastp - Preprocessing and trimming
  • Cutadapt - Adapter removal
  • MultiQC - Aggregate reports

docs.title: Bioinformatics CLI Tools Index
docs.text: A reference collection of command-line tools used in bioinformatics analysis pipelines.

docs.title: Quality Control & Preprocessing
docs.text: Tool Purpose FastQC Sequencing read quality assessment MultiQC Aggregate QC reports from multiple tools fastp All-in-one FASTQ preprocessing (trim + QC) Trimmomatic Flexible Illumina read trimming Cutadapt Adapter and primer removal

docs.title: Alignment — DNA
docs.text: Tool Purpose BWA Short-read alignment (Illumina WGS/WES standard) Bowtie2 Short-read alignment (ChIP-seq, general purpose) minimap2 Long-read alignment (PacBio, Nanopore) and assemblies

docs.title: Alignment — RNA-seq
docs.text: Tool Purpose STAR Ultrafast splice-aware RNA-seq aligner HISAT2 Graph-based splice-aware aligner (lower memory)

docs.title: Alignment Processing
docs.text: Tool Purpose samtools SAM/BAM/CRAM manipulation, sorting, indexing, filtering Picard Duplicate marking, BAM metrics, format validation

docs.title: Variant Calling & Manipulation
docs.text: Tool Purpose GATK Industry-standard germline/somatic variant calling bcftools Variant calling and VCF/BCF manipulation FreeBayes Bayesian haplotype-based variant caller VCFtools VCF filtering and population genetics statistics tabix Indexing and querying tab-delimited genomic files

docs.title: Quantification — RNA-seq
docs.text: Tool Purpose featureCounts Fast read counting for genomic features HTSeq-count Python-based read counting Kallisto Pseudoalignment transcript quantification Salmon Selective-alignment transcript quantification

docs.title: Coverage & Visualisation
docs.text: Tool Purpose deepTools Normalized BigWig tracks, heatmaps, QC plots bedtools Genomic interval arithmetic (intersect, merge, coverage)

docs.title: Sequence Search & Alignment
docs.text: Tool Purpose BLAST Local similarity search (nucleotide and protein) HMMER Profile HMM search (remote homology, domain annotation) MUSCLE Multiple sequence alignment MAFFT Multiple sequence alignment (scalable to large datasets)

docs.title: Sequence Manipulation
docs.text: Tool Purpose SeqKit FASTA/FASTQ toolkit (stats, grep, filter, convert)
